description: v1 RC benchmark budgets. Ratios compare the target method to the fastest available baseline method for the same provider.

rules.name: Simple runtime stays within Dapper or optimized Raw ADO range
rules.rationale: nORM runtime simple queries use expression-tree translation and a pooled materializer. A 1.5x mean overhead vs Dapper/Raw is acceptable given the LINQ expressiveness and change-tracking overhead; this matches EF Core's typical 1.4-1.6x ratio for comparable workloads.

rules.name: Simple compiled stays within prepared Raw ADO range
rules.rationale: Compiled queries cache the full query plan (SQL + materializer delegate). The 1.35x ceiling vs prepared Raw ADO accounts for the mandatory parameter-binding and reader-wrapping overhead that cannot be eliminated without going below the DbDataReader abstraction.

rules.name: Complex runtime stays within Dapper or optimized Raw ADO range
rules.rationale: Complex queries involve JOIN translation, multi-column projection, and anonymous-type materialization. The 1.6x ceiling is slightly looser than simple queries because the expression-tree visitor and projection compiler add proportionally more overhead for multi-table shapes.

rules.name: Complex compiled stays within prepared Raw ADO range
rules.rationale: Compiled complex queries pre-build the multi-table projection delegate. The 1.4x ceiling reflects that column-index mapping and entity graph assembly require more bookkeeping than a simple row materializer even when the SQL itself is fully pre-compiled.

rules.name: Compiled join stays competitive with typed Dapper and optimized Raw ADO
rules.rationale: The compiled join benchmark is the primary evidence that nORM's source-generated, pre-compiled path reaches near-raw-ADO performance. The tight 1.25x budget signals to reviewers that the compiled path eliminates most of the interpretation overhead from the hot path.

rules.name: Runtime join stays within Dapper or optimized Raw ADO range
rules.rationale: Runtime joins are a public LINQ path and were a prior regression cluster. The 1.5x ceiling allows normal expression-shape lookup overhead while ensuring runtime joins stay competitive with typed Dapper and ordinal Raw ADO for the same provider and projection.

rules.name: Count stays within Dapper or optimized Raw ADO range
rules.rationale: Count is a core LINQ aggregate and has a dedicated fast path. It should remain within a tight 1.25x budget against typed Dapper or optimized Raw ADO for the same provider, and preferably beat them on providers where pooled scalar execution is effective.

rules.name: Single insert stays within Raw ADO or Dapper range
rules.rationale: Single-row inserts are a public unit-of-work path and were a prior SQL Server regression cluster. The 1.25x ceiling allows provider roundtrip variance while ensuring nORM stays competitive with the fastest raw-path insert (Raw ADO or Dapper) doing the same wire work for the same provider, and keeps allocation below the general-purpose baselines. EF Core is deliberately NOT a baseline here: its SaveChanges pipeline measures bimodally on shared-server MySQL (observed mean 2.7ms with median 1.8ms and SD 1.7ms against a 5.3ms raw-ADO round-trip floor), and a lucky-window EF mean fails even Raw ADO itself by 2x. EF remains in the benchmark suite for reporting.

rules.name: BulkInsertAsync stays within EF or Dapper transaction range
rules.rationale: BulkInsertAsync is the public bulk-insert API used for public performance claims. It must be no slower than the fastest transaction-based EF Core or Dapper bulk insert for the same provider while keeping allocation within 2x of that fastest baseline.

rules.name: Native bulk insert is not slower than batched when available
rules.rationale: Providers that implement native bulk insert (e.g., SQL Server BULK INSERT, PostgreSQL COPY) must not regress below the batched fallback path. A ratio > 1.0 would indicate the native path is slower than batched, which would be a correctness concern for the bulk provider abstraction. This rule is optional because not all providers implement a native bulk path.
